coredata.dc:title: Surface adsorption of adenine on pristine and B/N/O/P-doped coronene as a biosensing substrate for DNA detection- DFT study
coredata.dc:creator: Aneesh Kumar, R.
Jamelah Al-Otaibi, S.
Sheena Mary, Y.
Shyma Mary, Y.
Acharjee, Nivedita
Thomas, Renjith
Raveendran Pillai, Renjith
Leena, T.L.
coredata.dc:description: Nucleobase detection is crucial for the exploration of specific nucleotide sequences. Inspired by the recent realization of two dimensional nanomaterials as DNA detectors as well as sensors, the interactions of pristine and B/N/O/P-doped coronenes with adenine nucleobase have been studied on the basis of Density Functional Theory. Optimal configurations, the corresponding adsorption energies, charge transfer and electrical properties have been calculated for each complex. The adsorption strength and charge transfer in doped coronene have been found larger than that in the pristine coronene. AIM-RDG study suggests that non-covalent interactions existing in the associated interactive region are responsible for the stability of the complexes. The change in electrical conductivity of coronenes after the adsorption indicates its sensitivity towards DNA bases. The predicted energy gap and the prolonged recovery time for adenine-coronene configurations imply that pristine/doped coronene has potential applications in DNA detection.

head.ce:title: Surface adsorption of adenine on pristine and B/N/O/P-doped coronene as a biosensing substrate for DNA detection- DFT study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nucleobase detection is crucial for the exploration of specific nucleotide sequences. Inspired by the recent realization of two dimensional nanomaterials as DNA detectors as well as sensors, the interactions of pristine and B/N/O/P-doped coronenes with adenine nucleobase have been studied on the basis of Density Functional Theory. Optimal configurations, the corresponding adsorption energies, charge transfer and electrical properties have been calculated for each complex. The adsorption strength and charge transfer in doped coronene have been found larger than that in the pristine coronene. AIM-RDG study suggests that non-covalent interactions existing in the associated interactive region are responsible for the stability of the complexes. The change in electrical conductivity of coronenes after the adsorption indicates its sensitivity towards DNA bases. The predicted energy gap and the prolonged recovery time for adenine-coronene configurations imply that pristine/doped coronene has potential applications in DNA detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Guanine and Adenine are two important purine nucleic acid bases of fundamental biochemical importance [1]. Alterations in these two nucleic acid bases weaken or mutate immune system, which can lead to a number of diseases including cancer, epilepsy, Parkinsonism, AIDS and liver disorders [2]. In the field of biomolecular analysis and disease diagnosis, detection of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) has great importance, since the detection of nucleic acids is crucial for the exploration of specific nucleotide sequences, the early diagnosis of a diseased state and the monitoring of its treatment [3]. Even though several analytical techniques such as fluorescence [4], gas chromatography [5], capillary zone electrophoresis [6] and infrared reflection absorption spectroscopy [7] have been developed for the detection of DNA or RNA, these methods are relatively slow. Compared to the above mentioned analytical techniques, electrochemical sensors are faster and less expensive for simple evaluation [8].

Carbon-based biosensors have been widely used due to their biocompatibility and low cost [9,10]. With the recent advances in nanotechnology, ultrahigh sensitive and specific detection of nucleic acids are possible with the help of two dimensional nanomaterials based bioassays [11], which have unique electronic, optical and mechanical properties [12,13]. From the previously reported studies, it has been understood that single nucleobase molecule detection using pure 2D nanomaterials has its own limitations. A large number of studies were carried out by modifying graphene surfaces to improve graphene sheet’s sensitivity towards biomolecules [14–17]. One of the most practical ways to alter the characteristics of graphene is heteroatom doping [18]. It has been reported that structural and electronic properties of carbon-based 2D nanomaterials are significantly improved by doping with heteroatoms such as B, Al, N and P [19–21]. Recently, several studies show that fullerene and ZnO nanoclusters are very effective as nanocarriers due to their distinct advantages [22–25].

Doping of graphene with boron atoms is getting wide attention because of the similarities among B and C atoms which permit the maintenance of planar structure of graphene. In the case of gaseous dopant, formation energy of boron-doped graphene sheet is much lower, which is of the order of 5.6 eV per atom [26]. Srivastava et al. [27] proposed and successfully synthesized a boron-doped few-layer graphene for intensive ammonia gas sensing and they have been found that it is highly stable. In the last few years, many experimental and theoretical studies based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) have been carried out to reveal the possibilities of boron and nitrogen doped graphene sheet as a very excellent candidate for gas sensing applications [28–31]. Shuyan Ge et al. [32] synthesized B-doped GQDs (Graphene Quantum Dots) using hydrothermal process, and permitted excellent absorption which made it suitable for biosensing application via cytochrome detection. Holey nitrogen-doped graphene aerogel synthesized via hydrothermal method which followed a high-temperature annealing in NH3-Ar mixed atmosphere was employed for the electrochemical determination of uric acid, dopamine and ascorbic acid [33]. An electrochemical biosensor based on N-doped porous graphene is also reported for the detection of femtomolar miRNA [34]. Nitrogen-doped Graphene sheet synthesized via Hummers oxidation method using graphite oxide as precursor was found to be the best performing material for the detection of biomolecules such as ascorbic acid, dopamine and NADH [35]. Since the donor capacity of phosphorous is much greater than that of nitrogen, it is anticipated that n-type nature of graphene could be elevated by phosphorous doping in pristine graphene. Chu and coworkers [36] synthesized P-doped graphene via thermal annealing method and used as metal catalyst support for the detection of dopamine. It has been found that doping of graphene with P leads to the enhancement of electrolytic activity of graphene towards dopamine oxidation. Recent studies show that the combination of highly interacting carbon nanomaterials and drug molecules could be used as drug delivery agents as well as sensors [37].

Following their own earlier studies, the present research group concentrate on the usage of pristine and doped graphene as possible adenine (HPA) carriers in this work [38–44]. In order to do this, coronene has been used as a model for graphene and doped it with B/N/P/O atoms in para places at the center of the ring. In the present work, the surface adsorption of adenine (HPA) on the pristine coronene (CC), B-doped CC (BBP), N-doped CC (NNP), O-doped CC (OOP) and P-doped CC (PPP) have been investigated. To find the adsorption ability of the pristine and doped coronene towards the adenine, the first principle calculations based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) has been applied. To gain better understanding on the adsorption behavior of adenine on the surface of the graphene sheets, adsorption energies, charge transfer, electron density, and electrostatic potential (ESP) maps have been analyzed. In order to obtain insight in to the adsorption properties and sensing mechanism of the modeled systems as biosensors and drug carriers, density of states (DOS), Frontier molecular orbitals, electrical conductivity and recovery time have been calculated.

The possible interaction between adenine and doped/pristine coronene was studied with the help of density functional theory (DFT) calculations using reliable B3LYP functional with basis set 6-31G*. To model the possible interactions between HPA drug molecule and graphene, five graphene based carriers such as, pristine graphene (coronene: CC) and B/N/P/O doped coronene sheets (BBP, NNP, PPP and OOP) were designed. Before optimizations at the DFT level, all structures were pre-optimized using the GFN2-xtb method implemented in the xtb program developed by Prof. Grimme and coworkers [45–48]. With B3LYP/6-31G* basis set and no symmetry constraints on the structures, geometry optimization was done. Calculations with the xtb program were performed by using the online xtb calculator at atomistica.online molecular modeling platform [49], available at https://atomistica.online. DFT calculations were carried out using Gaussian16 software [50,51]. Initially HPA and coronene suraces were parallel at a separation of 3 Å and then allowed to be optimized. Absence of imaginary frequencies during the simulation of vibration spectra suggests that all the systems belong to the energy minimum. The pristine coronene and doped coronenes with B/N/P/O at para positions at the center part of the coronene are shown in Fig. 1 with HOMO, LUMO and MEP plots. The optimized geometry of HPA, pristine/doped coronene with adenine are depicted in Fig. 2 . The adsorption energy (Ead ) for each HPA-Coronene complexes was calculated using the following expression:
Ead = E

EBSSE is the “basis set superposition error”, which is calculated by the counterpoise method to achieve highly accurate adsorption energy functional prediction [52]. The electrical conductivity changes of the pristine and doped coronene sheet before and after the adsorption are calculated using the equation,

where σ, is the electrical conductivity of a given configuration, E g is the energy gap, kB , is the Boltzmann constant and T, is the thermodynamic temperature [53]. Recovery time can be calculated using the equation

where ρ is the attempt frequency, kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the thermodynamic temperature and Ed is the desorption energy barrier, which is equal to the adsorption energy [54]. DOS plots were prepared and analyzed with the help of Gausssum suite [55]. The AIM [56,57] topological studies, NCI [58] and LOL were performed using Multiwfn software [59], and the NCI-RDG were visualized using VMD 1.9.3 software [60].

The calculated values of interaction energies (ΔE), BSSE correction (BSSE) and BSSE corrected interaction energies (ΔEBSSE) obtained at B3LYP/6-31G* level of theory are given in supplementary material as Table S1. From the Table S1, it can be seen that the difference between the BSSE corrected values and non-corrected values of adsorption energies are in the range 1.91–3.29 kcal/mol for HPA-CC, 2.12–3.57 kcal/mol for HPA-BBD, 2.42–3.67 kcal/mol for HPA-NNP, 2.10–3.09 kcal/mol for HPA-PPP and 2.34–3.62 kcal/mol for HPA-OOP complexes. Adenine and graphene interactions were assessed using adsorption energy (Eads) values given in Table 1
[61,62]. The improved adsorption ability is evident from the fact that the doped coronenes possess more negative adsorption energy values than the undoped coronenes. Adsorption energy of HPA-PPP system is found to be the configuration with the lowest energy (−274.79 kcal/mol). On the other hand, the adsorption energy of HPA adsorbed on the pristine coronene surface is predicted as −229.56 kcal/mol, which is much lower than the most stable configuration HPA-PPP. The adsorption energies of other configurations HPA-NNP, HPA-OOP and HPA-BBP are found as −269.38, −257.21 and −247.43 kcal/mol, respectively. The large negative values of adsorption energy for all configurations of HPA with coronene surfaces reveal that adenine adsorption on the pristine/doped coronene surfaces is an exothermic process and hence energetically favorable. This confirms that doped coronene sheets are better candidates for drug delivery systems than pristine coronene sheets.

Enthalpy values of all configurations are found to be negative, which confirms that HPA adsorption is an exothermic process (Table 2 ). Entropy drops for all complexes with adenine adsorption, which is due to a reduction in the degree of freedom for translation. The computed ΔG values for adsorption are negative, which can be seen in the Table 2 and the adsorption of HPA takes place spontaneously. As a result, it is anticipated that HPA will spontaneously bind to the coronene surface. For the doped coronene sheets, the changes in entropy are more negative with respect to pristine coronene (−27.160 kcal/mol K). For doped configurations, highest entropy change is (−31.761 kcal/mol K) for OOP-HPA complex. The enthalpy and Gibbs energy changes are more negative as in the case of entropy changes, with highest value for PPP-HPA (−277.10 and −268.43 kcal/mol K). These findings support the fact that HPA can be loaded successfully on the surface of coronenes [63]. In all cases, the HPA is nearly perpendicular to the undoped/doped coronene surfaces.

For PPP-HPA system, the N1H11, NH2 and C9H12 are interacted with the P-doped coronene surface with a separation of 2.8053, 2.6380 and 2.9606 Å. The C-P lengths in this configuration vary as 1.8277–1.8048 Å, while the CPC bond angle range is 100.3 to 102.8° and the P atoms are projected from the plane of the coronene system in a distorted manner. In pristine PPP system, the C-P bond length is in the range of 1.8503 to 1.8699 Å and CPC angle is from 99.5 to 102.1°. For OOP-HPA system, N1H11 and NH2 are interacted with the O-doped coronene at a distance of 2.5164 and 2.4847 Å. The C-O bond lengths of this complex vary as 1.4563–1.4510 Å, while the COC bond angle range is 116.9 to 117.0° and the O atoms are separated from the edge C atoms of the coronene ring. In pristine OOP system, C-O bond length is 1.4642 Å and COC angle is 119.7°. For NNP-HPA system, the N1H11, NH2 and C9H12 are interacted with the N-doped coronene at distances 2.4133, 2.6811 and 3.5302 Å. The C-N bond length in this complex varies as 1.3984–1.4385 Å, while the CNC bond angle range is 121.5 to 117.5°. In pristine NNP system, C-N bond length is in the range of 1.4251–1.4056 Å and CNC angle is from 119.7 to 120.1°.

For CC-HPA system, the N1H11 and NH2 are interacted with the pristine coronene at distances 2.6886 and 2.6759 Å. The C–C lengths in this complex are 1.4270 Å with CCC bond angle as 120.0°. In pristine CC system, C–C bond length is in the range of 1.4301–1.4247 Å and CCC angle is 120.0°. For BBP-HPA system, the NH2, N1H11 and C9H12 are interacted with the B-doped coronene at distances, 2.6323, 2.5728 and 3.2956 Å. The C-B lengths vary as 1.5352–1.5927 Å while the CBC bond angle range is 118.8 to 122.0°. In pristine BBP system, C-B bond length is in the range of 1.5223 to 1.5083 Å and CBC angle is from 119.3 to 121.3°. In all these cases, the interaction of adenine with pristine and doped coronenes are mainly through NH2 and N1H11 functional groups and the separation suggests a chemisorption process as in literature [64,65].

Table 1 shows that HPA and PPP coronenes are polar with dipole moments (DM) of 7.7742 and 3.1621 Debye respectively, while other coronenes are non-polar. DM of the complexes considerably increases after HPA is adsorbed on the surface of coronenes. The DM values vary in the order NNP-HPA (9.3083 Debye) > OOP-HPA (9.1704 Debye) > BBP-HPA (8.1604 Debye) > CC-HPA (8.5027 Debye) > PPP-HPA (5.5616 Debye) with maximum value for the adsorption in N-doped coronene surface. These findings suggest that HPA adsorption on coronene surfaces elevates the solubility of the complexes, which is a beneficial characteristic for drug delivery. The distribution of electron density in the investigated systems has been analyzed using the MEP (Fig. 3 ). The cationic environment is indicated by the positive MEP regions (blue), while the electronic charge accumulation is depicted by the negative regions (red). The adenine has positive potential on hydrogen atoms and negative potential mainly on N3 and N2 atoms, which may imply that the charge transfer had been occurred during the adsorption of HPA on GQD surfaces. It is evident that the electric charge is distributed asymmetrically throughout the surfaces of complexes, which is consistent with the findings on the dipole moments (Table 1).

The Frontier Molecular Orbitals (FMOs) are significant factors in determining electric and optical characteristics of any chemical processes [66]. In Fig. 4 , the HOMO and LUMO are shown. The FMOs in HPA are dispersed throughout the molecule. HOMO and LUMO are distributed respectively, on HPA and graphene which support the charge transfer in CC-HPA complex. In the HPA-BBP configuration, HOMO and LUMO are over the entire regions of HPA and coronenes, while for other doped N/P/O coronenes, the FMOs are only over the coronene’s surface with rearrangement in electron density. This redistribution of electron densities in all complexes supports the charge transfer during the adsorption process.

DOS spectra were estimated and displayed in Fig. 5 to provide more contexts to show how interactions alter the HOMO-LUMO energy gap. In the adsorbed complexes, there is an overlapping of orbitals in HOMO and LUMO regions showing the reduction in energy gaps (Table 3 ). Table 3 also includes the energies of the analyzed system’s FMOs, chemical potentials and energy gaps. Graphene’s HOMO and LUMO energies are −5.4879 and −1.4046 eV, respectively, with a gap of 4.0833 eV [67]. After doping. the pristine coronene’s energy gap changes as: BBP (0.3064) < NNP (0.3442) < OOP (1.4806) < PPP (2.0492) < CC (4.0833 eV) while that of HPA is 5.3704 eV. After HPA adsorption on coronene surfaces, the energy gap changes in the order: CC-HPA (3.9157) > PPP-HPA (2.0232) > NNP-HPA (1.1696) > BBP-HPA (1.1663) > OOP-HPA (1.5891). The DOS figure (Fig. 5) shows that once HPA was adsorbed on coronene surface, the energy gap of all complexes was reduced. In contrast to all other coronene systems, change in Eg is significant for CC-HPA configuration.

Reactivity descriptors were estimated to assess the reactivity of all systems (Table 3) [68]. With the adsorption of HPA on coronene surfaces, the chemical potential of HPA-CC/BBP/PPP complexes (-3.9092 eV/-5.0289 eV/-3.9664 eV) becomes more negative while the chemical potential of HPA-NNP/OOP complexes (-2.3017 eV/-2.9035 eV) becomes less negative when compared to the chemical potential for HPA (-3.5840 eV). The stability of a species can be determined by the hardness, which measures the chemical system’s resistance to changes in the electron cloud. High chemical reactivity is related to narrow energy gap, hardness and chemical potential values. The present findings show that due to the adsorption of HPA, hardness for HPA-coronene systems is reduced in the order: CC-HPA (1.9579 eV) > PPP-HPA (1.0116 eV) > OOP-HPA (0.7946 eV) > NNP-HPA (0.5848 eV) > BBP-HPA (0.5832 eV) while the hardness of HPA is 2.6852 eV. High chemical reactivity is correlated with the large value of global electrophilicity index (ω). The BBP-HPA complex has a small energy gap of 1.1663 eV, chemical potential of −5.0289 eV and hardness of 0.5832 eV as well as the highest electrophilicity index of 21.6815 eV, as shown in Table 3. The BBP-HPA complex, as shown in Table 3, has a small energy gap and chemical potential, as well as the highest electrophilicity index and thus it becomes a better candidate among the studied configurations as a carrier to deliver HPA to the target site.

Electronic spectra of HPA have been generated theoretically by using TD-DFT to analyze the adenine-coronene interactions. The comparison spectra and data are addressed in Fig. 6 and Table 4 , which shows that TD-DFT yield one absorption at 245.95 nm for HPA while two absorptions are found at 443.79 nm and 675.56 nm for PPP-HPA complex. For CC-HPA complex there is a shift to 275.14 nm while all other coronene-HPA complexes give higher values of absorption. Table 5 shows the Mulliken charges of the HPA’s atoms before and after adsorption. All the nitrogen atoms show significant changes in charges. The charge of N1 of HPA (-0.75) becomes less negative to −0.71 in all adsorbed coronene systems, while N2 (-0.36), N3 (-0.33), N4 (-0.42) and N5 (-0.76) shifted to more negative around −0.47, −0.47, −0.50 and −0.79 in all adsorbed complexes. The charges of C7, C8 and C10 increase after adsorption with coronene surfaces while that of C6 and C9 decrease after adsorption. The hydrogen atoms adjacent to the nanosurface also show a variation in the value due to chemisorption.

Fig. 7 shows the predicted Raman spectra of HPA before and after the adsorption which indicated that most of the signals are amplified after the adsorption which leads to SERS [69]. Also, the HPA’s inactive Raman modes are found to be active with the enhanced intensity after adsorption, especially the NH, NH2 and ring modes. It is found that the polarizability tensor increases in all doped systems (Table 1) due to SERS enhancement and subsequently, there is an increase in NLO activity of all complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Topological analysis of the AIM and characterization of the interactions
ce:section.ce:para:
The interatomic interactions between HPA and the cage structure of coronene can be predicted using the AIM (atoms-in molecules) study proposed by Bader and coworkers [56,57], by calculating the accumulation of the electron density (ED) ρ at the critical points (CPs) in the interacting region and the second derivative of electron density ρ, determined by the Hessian of ρ, and diagonalized into three eigen values. The sum of these three eigen values λ 1, λ 2 and λ 3 is the Laplacian of electron density (LED)
∇ 2
ρ
(
r c
)
, the sign of which identifies the shared (covalent) or closed shell (non-covalent) interactions at the observed BCPs. The non-covalent interactions (NCIs) [58] can be characterized and visualized with the help of AIM-RDG (reduced density gradient) isosurfaces. In the present study, BBP, CC, NNP, OOP and PPP show the presence of bond and ring critical points (BCPs and RCPs) in the interacting region between adenine and the coronene cage. The AIM-RDG isosurfaces are shown in Fig. 8 . The red coloured isosurfaces are related to strongly repulsive NCIs, while the green ones are associated with the weak NCIs. The cage structure in BBP shows six RCPs with ρ values between 0.018 and 0.020 au, positive LED between 0.104 and 0.154 au and the LOL values between 0.121 and 0.139 au. The electron density ρ accumulated at the six RCPs associated with the cage in CCP, NNP, OOP and PPP differs minimally from BBP and they are calculated as 0.018–0.019 au in CCP and NNP, 0.016–0.019 in OOP and 0.015–0.020 in PPP, while the positive LED
∇ 2
ρ
(
r c
)
values in BBP, CCP, NNP, OOP and PPP differ appreciably and are calculated as 0.104–0.154 au, 0.139–0.148 au, 0.133–0.152 au, 0.115–0.152 au and 0.076–0.154 au respectively. The localized orbital locator (LOL) values at the RCPs associated with the cage in BBP, CCP, NNP, OOP and PPP are calculated respectively as 0.121–0.139, 0.121–0.122, 0.119–0.120, 0.116–0.122 and 0.123–0.138 au. This indicates the influence of the atoms on the associated repulsive NCIs. The HPA rings show the presence of two RCPs, with the accumulated total ED of 0.052 au and 0.024 au, the positive LED of 0.411 and 0.186 and the LOL of 0.187 and 0.137 au respectively, characterized as strongly repulsive NCIs (red colour) in the AIM-RDG isosurface. All five structures show the presence of two RCPs between HPA and the cage structure, each associated with weak NCIs. The RCPs show electron densities between 0.050 and 0.080 au, positive LED between 0.008 and 0.010 au and the LOL between 0.050 and 0.080 au. In addition to the RCPs, BBP and PPP show three BCPs, while CCP, NNP and OOP show two BCPs between adenine and the cage molecule. These BCPs found in the interactive region show the total ED between 0.003 and 0.013, positive LED of 0.008 and 0.037 au and LOL of 0.092 and 0.195 au, and they are found to be weak NCIs. Hence AIM-RDG study suggests that there exists no covalent interaction between adenine and the cage molecule in all these five structures, while non-covalent interactions existing predominantly in the associated interactive region are responsible for the stability of the complexes [70,71].

To find the use of HPA adsorption on pristine/doped coronene, the adsorption energies and thermodynamic properties are calculated in different solvents such as acetonitrile, DMSO, methanol, nitromethane and water. In all these solvents, the adsorption energy becomes more negative in comparison with that in vacuum. Maximum adsorption energies of all HPA-Coronene complexes are found in water, when compared to other polar solvents. (Table 1). This may be attributed to the large dipole moments of the HPA-complex systems in water and hence, compared to other polar solvents, the contribution of hydrophobic forces in water elevates the stability of the HPA-coronene complexes. Since the order of ΔH and ΔG are more negative in all solvent media (Table 2), it is evident that the adsorption is a spontaneous process. The ΔG for the doped systems show that HPA adsorption occurs spontaneously whereas the ΔG value is less for the CC system. The exothermic nature of HPA adsorption is further supported by negative values of ΔH [72,73]. The chemical descriptors in solvent media also show variations with respect to the corresponding values in vacuum (Table 3). The energy gap and hardness increase for all complexes except BBP-HPA complex for which these values decrease. The chemical potential becomes less negative for all complexes except for NNP-HPA. The BBP-HPA shows high values of electrophilicity index both in vacuum and in all solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electrical properties of the HPA-Coronene adsorption system
ce:section.ce:para: Electrical properties of the HPA-coronene systems were calculated in order to study the sensitivity of pristine/doped coronene sheets towards adenine. One of the important parameters which determines the efficiency of detection of biomolecules by coronene sheet is electrical conductivity, which is different before and after interaction with the biomolecules. The energy gap plays a crucial role in determining the conductivity of graphene nanosheets. In order to discuss the possibility of using the pristine/doped coronene as a better sensor for DNA, electrical properties of the adsorption system are to be calculated. The calculated values of energy gap (Eg ) and the corresponding values of electrical conductivity (σ) are presented in Table S2. From the table, it can be seen that after adsorption of adenine, the electrical conductivity of pristine CC and P-doped coronene surface increased, while the conductivity of other doped coronenes like, OOP, NNP and BBP decreased. After adsorption, large changes in the electrical conductivity could be observed for BBP and NNP surfaces when compared to pristine and other doped coronene surfaces. Though the change in the conductivity of pristine CC, PPP and OOP surfaces is small when compared to those of other configurations, it is significant when it comes to the detection of DNA. This shows that, the pristine/doped coronene can be potentially used as a bio-sensing substrate for DNA detection.

In order to validate the reusability of pristine/doped coronene monolayer as a DNA-sensitive material, recovery time is to be calculated. Recovery time is a parameter which is commonly used to determine the quality of the biosensor device, which is experimentally determined by heating the substrate, while theoretically calculated by transition state theory based approach [74–76]. Recovery time can be calculated using the equation
τ =
ρ
- 1
e x p
- E
d
/
k B
T
Where ρ is the attempt frequency, kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the thermodynamic temperature and Ed is the desorption energy barrier, which is equal to the adsorption energy [54].

From this equation, it is understood that the more negative is the adsorption energy value, the longer is the recovery time. Since the calculated adsorption energies of all the configurations are very large, the recovery time is very long, which ensures adequate time for adenine molecule to interact with pristine/doped coronene surfaces. The longer recovery time for all HPA-pristine/doped coronene configurations suggests that it takes years to recover and the desorption process is difficult. This prevents the applications of the pristine/doped coronene sheet as a reusable sensor for adenine base, but promotes its use as a disposable DNA base detector.

Density Functional Theory calculations have been used to study the interaction of an important nucleic acid base, adenine with pristine and B/N/O/P-doped coronene surface. The calculated values of adsorption energy and thermodynamical parameters show that adsorption strength of doped coronene-HPA systems is higher than that of pristine coronene. Due to the adsorption of adenine, an elevation was observed in the dipole moment of HPA-coronene configurations and it resulted in an increase in the solubility of complexes, which itself is an essential characteristics of effective drug delivery. All complexes have higher polarizabilities than HPA and pristine/doped coronene and it suggests an increase in charge transfer with high NLO activity. After adsorption, large changes in the electrical conductivity could be observed for BBP and NNP surfaces compared to pristine and other doped coronene surfaces. This shows that, the B/P doped coronene can be potentially used as a bio-sensing substrate for DNA detection. The prolonged recovery time for all adenine-pristine/doped coronene configurations indicates that pristine/doped coronene can be used as a disposable DNA base detector.
